title: Learning Interpretable Dynamics from Images of a Freely Rotating 3D Rigid Body

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper propose a method to learn dynamics and make predictions from sequences of images of 3D rigid bodies. The method incorporates physics priors by using SO(3) as the latent space, and by translating the problem of learning the dynamics to that of learning a (reduced) Hamiltonian. Predictions are then made by and integrating the kinematic equations on SO(3), and Lie-Poisson equations on the Lie coalgebra of SO(3).

They perform experiments with a synthetic dataset consisting of sequences of rotating rigid bodies with different shapes and different, diagonal and non-diagonal, inertia tensors.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The methods makes good use of well-known classical mechanics results about freely rotating rigid bodies giving good theoretical reasons for why the method should work. Interpretability of the learned dynamics is of utmost importance when using machine learning to model dynamical systems and the proposed algorithm gives very interpretable results constraining the dynamics on SO(3) and leaving all the unknown and irrelevant characteristics, such as color and shape of the modelled object, to the autoencoder.

Unfortunately, as stated in the paper, there aren't many datasets to assess performances for the particular problem tackled in this paper. 
The dataset could also represent a good benchmark for future works if made available, and therefore is a notable contribution.
The comparison with LSTMs and Neural ODEs is really meaningful as these methods are not as domain-specific as the proposed algorithms but certainly show how inductive biases like the one proposed in this paper are the way to go. Is there any 

Apart from the dynamics, the SO(3) latent space seems to facilitate the reconstruction: perhaps a pre-training of just the encoder-decoder architecture, which is not unrealistic for real-world application, would boost performances. Or perhaps you already do that.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The background section is very concise but sufficiently clear for readers that are familiar with Hamiltonian formulation of rigid bodies dynamics.
As claimed by the authors, and to the best of my knowledge it is the first physics-informed deep learning algorithm for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid bodies.

A few questions:

How figure 4 should be interpreted? I struggle to understand why the predicted $S^{2}\times S^{2}$ parameterisation of SO(3) is so different from the ground truth. In this setting there is a lot of prior knowledge used in the algorithm, namely we know that the system is a freely rotating rigid body. Therefore, as you emphasise, "learning dynamics" boils down to making good predictions. In figure 3 I observe seemingly good predictions that I can't really conciliate with figure 4, where trajectories clearly differ. Section 6.2 does not provide sufficient explanation for why this is the case.

Because of the freedom os assigning an inertial frame Eq. (4) (and the corresponding Lie-Poisson) and (2) are independent. It is not very clear why you need the latter system of equations? Technically you could perform predictions using only the quaternion equations. Is it only because you want to enforce energy conservation? If so it would be interesting to know how the algorithm performs without this penalty in the loss.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper propose a method to learn dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies based on the simple idea that in the Hamiltonian framework, rigid bodies dynamics happens on $T^{*}SO(3)$. This makes learned dynamics interpretable and is a concrete step towards more trustworthy, less black-box machine learning models. Despite I asked for a few clarifications, I think the paper is overall clear.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Paper Summary

This work presents an approach for learning the dynamics of 3D rigid bodies from image sequences by leveraging SO(3) as the latent representation space. The key innovation lies in incorporating classical mechanics principles, specifically reformulating the dynamics learning problem as learning a reduced Hamiltonian function. Predictions are subsequently generated by integrating kinematic equations on SO(3) and Lie-Poisson equations on the corresponding Lie coalgebra. The evaluation is conducted on a synthetic dataset featuring rotating rigid bodies with varying geometries and inertia tensor properties.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

A significant strength of this work is its principled use of established classical mechanics theory regarding freely rotating rigid bodies, providing solid theoretical foundations for the proposed approach. The emphasis on interpretability is particularly commendable—by constraining the learned dynamics to the SO(3) manifold and delegating the capture of irrelevant features (such as color and shape) to an autoencoder, the method achieves a transparent representation of the underlying physics that practitioners can understand and validate.

The paper acknowledges a notable limitation: the scarcity of existing benchmarks for this specific problem domain. The authors' synthetic dataset could serve as a valuable resource for future research if released publicly, representing a meaningful contribution in itself. The empirical comparison against LSTMs and Neural ODEs is particularly valuable, as it effectively demonstrates the advantages of incorporating domain-specific inductive biases rather than relying on generic sequence modeling approaches.

One aspect worth investigating further is whether pre-training the encoder-decoder architecture independently—a practical consideration for real-world applications—could enhance reconstruction quality. The SO(3) parameterization appears to offer benefits for reconstruction beyond just dynamics learning, and exploring this separately could yield insights.

## Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

The background material is appropriately concise while remaining sufficiently rigorous for readers with knowledge of Hamiltonian mechanics. To the best of my knowledge, this represents the first physics-informed deep learning method specifically designed for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid bodies, constituting a novel contribution to the field.

Several clarifications would strengthen the paper:

1. **Figure 4 interpretation**: The discrepancy between predicted and ground truth S²×S² parameterizations of SO(3) in Figure 4 is puzzling and difficult to reconcile with the seemingly accurate predictions shown in Figure 3. Given the substantial prior knowledge embedded in the algorithm—namely, that the system represents a freely rotating rigid body—one would expect closer alignment between predicted and ground truth parameterizations. The explanation provided in Section 6.2 appears insufficient to account for this divergence.

2. **Role of dual representations**: The freedom in selecting an inertial frame makes Equations (4) and (2) technically independent. The motivation for employing both the quaternion-based kinematic equations and the Lie-Poisson formulation remains unclear. Is the primary rationale energy conservation enforcement? It would be informative to understand how performance degrades if this constraint is removed from the loss function, helping to quantify its practical contribution.

## Overall Assessment

The paper presents a well-motivated approach to learning rigid body dynamics by anchoring the problem within the Hamiltonian framework, where dynamics naturally evolve on T*SO(3). This design choice successfully renders the learned dynamics interpretable and represents a meaningful step toward developing more trustworthy machine learning models that operate less as black boxes. While the review raised several questions requiring clarification, the paper is generally well-presented and clearly communicated.

## Recommendation

**8: Accept, good paper**

In this setting there is a lot of prior knowledge used in the algorithm, namely we know that the system is a freely rotating rigid body. Therefore, as you emphasise, "learning dynamics" boils down to making good predictions. In figure 3 I observe seemingly good predictions that I can't really conciliate with figure 4, where trajectories clearly differ. Section 6.2 does not provide sufficient explanation for why this is the case.

Because of the freedom os assigning an inertial frame Eq. (4) (and the corresponding Lie-Poisson) and (2) are independent. It is not very clear why you need the latter system of equations? Technically you could perform predictions using only the quaternion equations. Is it only because you want to enforce energy conservation? If so it would be interesting to know how the algorithm performs without this penalty in the loss.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper propose a method to learn dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies based on the simple idea that in the Hamiltonian framework, rigid bodies dynamics happens on $T^{*}SO(3)$. This makes learned dynamics interpretable and is a concrete step towards more trustworthy, less black-box machine learning models. Despite I asked for a few clarifications, I think the paper is overall clear.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper is definitely interesting. My only concern is the lack of evaluation on real data and the inability of method to deal with non-uniform data. 

After reading the rebuttal and discussion with other reviewers, I have changed my opinion (a bit unfavorably). Here are the main concerns:

1. The experimentation is highly limited. The discussion on handling non-linear mass distribution is non-existent. The authors provide some explanation in the rebuttal but a lot questions remain. Given that the setup is difficult, perhaps the authors could explore non-linearity a bit more. For example, how does the method behave when the mass is non-uniform in a way that the centre of mass deviates strongly with respect to object centre. This discussion could have helped to understand the practical limitations of the method better. Plus, the authors must experiment with more data. Right now, the datasets are limited to a cube and a prism.

2. There were some gaps in writing. The figure 2 in initial draft, which shows the trajectory to drift apart drastically, was later on removed. It is unclear to the readers how the registration works if the estimation of trajectory is so wrong. It is not clear whether the authors are performing a global alignment to fit into the projection. Perhaps the authors should have clarified.

Overall, the paper has an important contribution over sota but the technical novelty is limited. Without a strong experimental evaluation, it was difficult to have an undoubted positive opinion.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Overview

This paper proposes a novel approach for estimating 3D rotational dynamics of objects from image sequences, with specific application to satellite pose estimation. The core innovation lies in leveraging Hamiltonian mechanics to represent rotational motion, which offers the advantage of continuous differentiability across the entire trajectory space—a property not guaranteed by alternative parameterizations such as exponential maps or quaternions. The authors introduce a synthetic dataset comprising uniformly and non-uniformly rotating geometric primitives and develop a framework that encodes image sequences into a learned latent representation of rotational states, from which angular velocities are estimated and future frames are predicted via Hamiltonian dynamics.

## Strengths

1. **Novel Application of Hamiltonian Mechanics**: The integration of Hamiltonian dynamics into the satellite rotation estimation problem represents a meaningful contribution. This theoretical foundation provides mathematical rigor and addresses known discontinuity issues present in competing rotation parameterization schemes.

2. **Dataset Contribution**: The authors have constructed a dedicated 3D synthetic dataset to facilitate learning of rotational dynamics from visual observations, which supports reproducibility and provides a resource for future work.

3. **Empirical Performance**: The experimental results on the synthetic benchmark demonstrate reasonable predictive accuracy and suggest the method's viability in controlled settings.

## Weaknesses

1. **Absence of Real-World Validation**: A critical limitation is the complete lack of evaluation on actual observational data. The method has only been tested on synthetic imagery, leaving significant uncertainty about its practical applicability and robustness to real-world imaging conditions, noise, and artifacts.

2. **Trajectory Prediction Accuracy Issues**: Figure 4 reveals concerning behavior where rotational trajectory estimates diverge substantially from ground truth, despite reasonably accurate image predictions. The relationship between the short-term image predictions shown in Figure 3 (up to t=50) and the longer-term trajectory shown in Figure 4 requires clarification. This discrepancy raises questions about error accumulation and the reliability of the angular velocity estimates.

3. **Limited Experimental Scope**: The experimental evaluation is restricted to only two geometric shapes (cube and prism). This narrow scope is insufficient to establish the method's generalization capabilities or to thoroughly investigate its behavior under varying physical conditions.

4. **Inadequate Treatment of Non-Uniform Mass Distribution**: The paper provides minimal discussion of how the method handles non-uniform mass distributions. While some explanation was offered in the rebuttal, fundamental questions remain unaddressed. Specifically, the paper lacks investigation into scenarios where the center of mass deviates significantly from the geometric center—a condition relevant to real satellites. Such analysis would be valuable for understanding practical limitations.

5. **Clarity Regarding Registration and Alignment**: The original submission contained Figure 2, which illustrated substantial trajectory drift, but this figure was subsequently removed. This omission creates ambiguity about how the image registration process functions when trajectory estimation is demonstrably inaccurate. It remains unclear whether the authors employ global alignment procedures to reconcile projection mismatches. This technical detail should be explicitly clarified.

## Assessment of Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility

The writing is generally comprehensible, with the main concepts adequately explained. The authors appropriately identify limitations and future research directions. The commitment to release code upon acceptance supports reproducibility. The theoretical contribution is novel within the satellite dynamics estimation context; however, the technical novelty is somewhat limited, as the primary contribution amounts to applying an established mathematical framework to a new problem domain.

## Summary

The paper addresses an important problem and proposes a theoretically motivated solution. However, several significant concerns limit its contribution. The restriction to synthetic data, combined with limited experimental diversity and insufficient exploration of non-uniform mass distribution effects, undermines confidence in the method's practical utility. The apparent discrepancy between image prediction accuracy and trajectory estimation accuracy, coupled with unresolved questions about the registration mechanism, suggests the work requires substantial additional development before publication.

## Recommendation

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

The paper makes a meaningful contribution to the field but falls short of the acceptance bar due to limited experimental validation, narrow scope of evaluation, and unresolved technical concerns. Significant revisions addressing real-world applicability, expanded experimental coverage, and clearer technical exposition would be necessary to bring this work to publication standard.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This work presents a physics-informed neural network approach for estimating and predicting three-dimensional rotational dynamics from image sequences. The methodology employs a multi-stage pipeline that: (1) encodes individual images into a latent representation that is homeomorphic to SO(3), (2) derives angular velocities from pairs of latent representations, and (3) forecasts future latent states by leveraging Hamiltonian equations of motion with a learned Hamiltonian representation.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**

+ The integration of physics-based constraints into a deep learning framework for predicting image sequences of rigid bodies, achieved through embedding images as measurements within the SO(3) configuration space and advancing Hamiltonian dynamics in time.

+ Introduction of a novel dataset featuring rotating rigid bodies, including sequences of rotating cubes and rectangular prisms with varying density distributions.

**Weaknesses:**

- The experimental validation is limited to highly controlled, idealized scenarios. As acknowledged by the authors, this restriction significantly undermines the credibility of the conclusions drawn from the current experimental and ablation study results.

- The proposed method requires system-specific model training, limiting its generalizability across different physical systems.

- The second paragraph of the Summary section makes claims about contributions that lack adequate empirical or theoretical support.

## Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

**Clarity:** The introduction would benefit from more comprehensive exposition of the methodological approach. Additionally, Figure 1 requires clarification regarding what panels (a), (b), and (c) represent, as their meaning is currently ambiguous.

**Quality and Novelty:** Given that the authors themselves acknowledge the restriction to idealized experimental conditions, the scope of the experimental evaluation and ablation studies appears insufficient to substantiate the claims made in the paper.

**Reproducibility:** The manuscript lacks sufficient implementation details necessary for independent reproduction of the proposed method.

## Summary of Review

This paper proposes a physics-informed neural network for predicting three-dimensional rotational dynamics from image sequences. Nevertheless, the experimental validation and ablation studies in their current form do not provide adequate evidence to support the paper's claims. The novelty and advantages of the proposed approach relative to existing methods require more rigorous justification.

## Rating

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The manuscript proposes a method for estimating and predicting 3D rotation dynamics from image sequences of freely rotating 3D rigid bodies such as satellites. The proposed method maps individual images to latent representations isomorphic to SO(3) using auto encoder type neural networks, calculates angular velocities from the latent pairs, and then leams the moment of inertia tensor J in the Hamiltonian. Based on this prediction of future latent states, a predictive image is constructed using the auto encoder. After constructing a new rotating rigid body dataset consisting of a sequence of rotating cubes and rectangles, the authors show that the proposed method gives higher performance than LSTM and NeuralODE.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths

This research is impressive because it is a challenging task to realize a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video images. The fact that they have succeeded in predicting and reconstructing video images to some extent by incorporating a physical inductive bias specific to rotational motion into the model is also highly novel.



Weaknesses

The following two points are considered weaknesses


The advantage over previous research is unclear:

Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN) [1] has been shown to be able to learn up to 400 steps in the future for a two-body problem [2].
HGN is also more general-purpose than the proposed method.
The authors need to show that the proposed method has better prediction performance for videos than HGN.

[1] Peter Toth, Danilo J. Rezende, Andrew Jaegle, Sébastien Racanière, Aleksandar Botev, Irina Higgins, "Hamiltonian Generative Networks," ICLR 2020, 2019.

[2] Irina Higgins, Peter Wirnsberger, Andrew Jaegle, Aleksandar Botev, "SyMetric: Measuring the Quality of Learnt Hamiltonian Dynamics Inferred from Vision," NeurIPS 2021, 2021.



The purpose of the method is unclear:.

The author writes that the application of the study is to estimate the orientation of satellites, but the training data in this study assigns a different color to each side of the object. I think it does not match the configuration of the satellites. If the differences between each side of a satellite can be determined, the orientation and motion of a satellite with a known geometry can be estimated analytically. Therefore, the proposed method is not considered necessary to achieve the objectives of the study.

Also, the work [Duong and Atanasov (2021)] cited by the authors in section "2.3 LEARNING DYNAMICS FOR RIGID BODIES" to achieve more general learning of rigid body motions by estimating inverse mass properties could be used for satellite orientation estimation, but no comparative experiments have been conducted. 

These factors made me think that the relationship between the proposed method and the purpose of the study did not seem appropriate.

The manuscript proposes a method for estimating and predicting 3D rotation dynamics from image sequences of freely rotating 3D rigid bodies such as satellites. The proposed method maps individual images to latent representations isomorphic to SO(3) using auto encoder type neural networks, calculates angular velocities from the latent pairs, and then leams the moment of inertia tensor J in the Hamiltonian. Based on this prediction of future latent states, a predictive image is constructed using the auto encoder. After constructing a new rotating rigid body dataset consisting of a sequence of rotating cubes and rectangles, the authors show that the proposed method gives higher performance than LSTM and NeuralODE.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths

This research is impressive because it is a challenging task to realize a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video images. The fact that they have succeeded in predicting and reconstructing video images to some extent by incorporating a physical inductive bias specific to rotational motion into the model is also highly novel.



Weaknesses

The following two points are considered weaknesses


The advantage over previous research is unclear:

Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN) [1] has been shown to be able to learn up to 400 steps in the future for a two-body problem [2].
HGN is also more general-purpose than the proposed method.
The authors need to show that the proposed method has better prediction performance for videos than HGN.

[1] Peter Toth, Danilo J. Rezende, Andrew Jaegle, Sébastien Racanière, Aleksandar Botev, Irina Higgins, "Hamiltonian Generative Networks," ICLR 2020, 2019.

[2] Irina Higgins, Peter Wirnsberger, Andrew Jaegle, Aleksandar Botev, "SyMetric: Measuring the Quality of Learnt Hamiltonian Dynamics Inferred from Vision," NeurIPS 2021, 2021.



The purpose of the method is unclear:.

The author writes that the application of the study is to estimate the orientation of satellites, but the training data in this study assigns a different color to each side of the object. I think it does not match the configuration of the satellites. If the differences between each side of a satellite can be determined, the orientation and motion of a satellite with a known geometry can be estimated analytically. Therefore, the proposed method is not considered necessary to achieve the objectives of the study.

Also, the work [Duong and Atanasov (2021)] cited by the authors in section "2.3 LEARNING DYNAMICS FOR RIGID BODIES" to achieve more general learning of rigid body motions by estimating inverse mass properties could be used for satellite orientation estimation, but no comparative experiments have been conducted. 

These factors made me think that the relationship between the proposed method and the purpose of the study did not seem appropriate.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
quality

Some variables with hat are not defined.


clarity

Symbols and variables in chapter 3 were defined in chapter 4, which made the paper difficult for me to understand.


originality

Since there are already several methods for learning Hamiltonian systems from video images[1,2], it is difficult to say that the proposed method is novel unless its performance is sufficiently high.

Summary Of The Review:
The importance of the research is high because it is a challenging task to realize a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video images. However, the performance of the method has not been compared to similar more general-purpose methods, which makes its effectiveness questionable. Therefore, I believe this concern needs to be resolved to accept this manuscript.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary of the Manuscript

This paper introduces an approach for estimating and forecasting 3D rotational dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies (such as satellites) from video sequences. The core methodology involves encoding individual frames into latent representations that correspond to SO(3) using an autoencoder architecture, deriving angular velocities from consecutive latent embeddings, and subsequently learning the moment of inertia tensor J within a Hamiltonian framework. The model then generates future latent states and reconstructs corresponding video frames using the decoder. The authors introduce a new dataset featuring rotating cubes and rectangles, demonstrating superior performance compared to baseline approaches including LSTM and NeuralODE.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

### Strengths

The work tackles a genuinely difficult problem—developing a neural network-based approach that learns physics-informed models capable of generating realistic video predictions. The integration of rotational motion-specific physical constraints as inductive biases into the neural architecture is conceptually innovative and represents a meaningful contribution to physics-informed machine learning.

### Weaknesses

Two significant concerns substantially limit the contribution:

**1. Insufficient Differentiation from Existing Methods:**

The novelty and practical advantage of this approach relative to established techniques remains ambiguous. Specifically:
- Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN) [1] have demonstrated the capacity to predict dynamics over 400 timesteps for systems like two-body problems [2]
- HGN offers greater generality and flexibility compared to the proposed rotation-specific method
- The manuscript lacks direct empirical comparisons showing that this method achieves superior video prediction accuracy relative to HGN

This absence of comparative evaluation against the most relevant existing work undermines claims of methodological advancement.

[1] Peter Toth, Danilo J. Rezende, Andrew Jaegle, Sébastien Racanière, Aleksandar Botev, Irina Higgins, "Hamiltonian Generative Networks," ICLR 2020, 2019.

[2] Irina Higgins, Peter Wirnsberger, Andrew Jaegle, Aleksandar Botev, "SyMetric: Measuring the Quality of Learnt Hamiltonian Dynamics Inferred from Vision," NeurIPS 2021, 2021.

**2. Disconnect Between Proposed Method and Stated Application:**

A fundamental mismatch exists between the purported application and the experimental validation:
- The authors motivate their work around satellite orientation estimation, yet their training data utilizes distinctly colored faces on synthetic objects—a configuration inconsistent with actual satellite surfaces
- For real satellites with known geometric properties and distinguishable surface features, orientation and motion can be determined through analytical methods, rendering the learned approach unnecessary
- The cited work by Duong and Atanasov (2021) on learning rigid body dynamics through inverse mass property estimation appears directly applicable to the satellite orientation problem, yet no experimental comparison is provided

This misalignment raises questions about the practical relevance and necessity of the proposed method for its stated objectives.

## Quality, Clarity, Originality, and Reproducibility

**Quality:** Several variables denoted with hats lack proper definition.

**Clarity:** The organizational structure impedes readability—notation and variable definitions introduced in Chapter 4 are referenced in Chapter 3, creating confusion for readers.

**Originality:** Given that multiple established methods for learning Hamiltonian dynamics from visual data already exist [1,2], the novelty of this contribution cannot be adequately established without demonstrating sufficiently superior empirical performance.

## Overall Assessment

While the research addresses an intellectually challenging problem and demonstrates genuine innovation in incorporating rotational physics as a structural constraint, critical gaps remain unresolved. Most notably, the work lacks empirical validation against comparable, general-purpose baselines, making it difficult to assess whether the proposed specialization yields meaningful practical advantages. Additionally, the disconnect between the application context (satellite orientation) and the experimental setup raises concerns about the method's actual utility for its intended purpose. These issues must be addressed before the manuscript can be considered suitable for publication.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)**

The manuscript requires substantial revisions addressing the comparative evaluation gap and clarifying the relationship between the methodology and its proposed applications before it would warrant acceptance.

The manuscript proposes a method for estimating and predicting 3D rotation dynamics from image sequences of freely rotating 3D rigid bodies such as satellites. The proposed method maps individual images to latent representations isomorphic to SO(3) using auto encoder type neural networks, calculates angular velocities from the latent pairs, and then leams the moment of inertia tensor J in the Hamiltonian. Based on this prediction of future latent states, a predictive image is constructed using the auto encoder. After constructing a new rotating rigid body dataset consisting of a sequence of rotating cubes and rectangles, the authors show that the proposed method gives higher performance than LSTM and NeuralODE.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths

This research is impressive because it is a challenging task to realize a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video images. The fact that they have succeeded in predicting and reconstructing video images to some extent by incorporating a physical inductive bias specific to rotational motion into the model is also highly novel.



Weaknesses

The following two points are considered weaknesses


The advantage over previous research is unclear:

Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN) [1] has been shown to be able to learn up to 400 steps in the future for a two-body problem [2].
HGN is also more general-purpose than the proposed method.
The authors need to show that the proposed method has better prediction performance for videos than HGN.

[1] Peter Toth, Danilo J. Rezende, Andrew Jaegle, Sébastien Racanière, Aleksandar Botev, Irina Higgins, "Hamiltonian Generative Networks," ICLR 2020, 2019.

[2] Irina Higgins, Peter Wirnsberger, Andrew Jaegle, Aleksandar Botev, "SyMetric: Measuring the Quality of Learnt Hamiltonian Dynamics Inferred from Vision," NeurIPS 2021, 2021.



The purpose of the method is unclear:.

The author writes that the application of the study is to estimate the orientation of satellites, but the training data in this study assigns a different color to each side of the object. I think it does not match the configuration of the satellites. If the differences between each side of a satellite can be determined, the orientation and motion of a satellite with a known geometry can be estimated analytically. Therefore, the proposed method is not considered necessary to achieve the objectives of the study.

Also, the work [Duong and Atanasov (2021)] cited by the authors in section "2.3 LEARNING DYNAMICS FOR RIGID BODIES" to achieve more general learning of rigid body motions by estimating inverse mass properties could be used for satellite orientation estimation, but no comparative experiments have been conducted. 

These factors made me think that the relationship between the proposed method and the purpose of the study did not seem appropriate.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
quality

Some variables with hat are not defined.


clarity

Symbols and variables in chapter 3 were defined in chapter 4, which made the paper difficult for me to understand.


originality

Since there are already several methods for learning Hamiltonian systems from video images[1,2], it is difficult to say that the proposed method is novel unless its performance is sufficiently high.

Summary Of The Review:
The importance of the research is high because it is a challenging task to realize a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video images. However, the performance of the method has not been compared to similar more general-purpose methods, which makes its effectiveness questionable. Therefore, I believe this concern needs to be resolved to accept this manuscript.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Unveiling Transformers with LEGO: A Synthetic Reasoning Task

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a synthetic task that aims to capture aspects of multi-hop reasoning, and uses it to characterize the behavior of Transformer models. It finds that pre-trained transformers learn the task more rapidly, and attributes this to specific patterns of attention in the network. Introducing mechanisms that emulate these specific attention patterns helps close this gap in performance without the need for expensive pre-training. Models struggle with extrapolating to longer chains of reasoning than required during training. This deterioration can be mitigated by repeating layers with the same parameters as well as training with stochastic depth.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The proposed task is useful as a tool for understanding some ways in which a Transformer can perform multi-hop reasoning
- The proposed operationalization of requiring length extrapolation is effective at disentangling issues with positional encoding from the question of whether the model can extrapolate to longer chains of reasoning 
- Experiments help provide an understanding for how pre-training, as well as stochastic depth, help improve certain aspects of the models' capabilities
- Hardcoded attention patterns are shown to be just as effective for solving this specific task
- The writing of the paper is clear and well-structured

Weaknesses:
- One of the strengths of the attention mechanism is that it can learn any attention pattern a task may require, and the multi-head version can learn when (at what layer) and how much to use each pattern. The hardcoded attention patterns seem sufficient for the proposed synthetic task -- almost by construction of the LEGO task. There are some preliminary findings that they may also be effective for masked language modeling and at least SQuAD, but this direction is underexplored and perhaps not all of the necessary components for real NLP tasks are captured.
- The proposed task is perhaps not the best test-bed of *pre-training* specifically, because the approach used is to pre-train on natural language but then test on synthetic expressions. It would not be surprising for language-specific reasoning to not undergo transfer in this case, potentially leaving only the most general of structural patterns, like the attention patterns revealed in this work.
- Overall, it's not clear how much of the Transformers' capabilities are stressed by the proposed synthetic task. It could be that understanding multi-hop computation in the style of the LEGO task is the key for explaining current behaviors and proposing improvements. Alternatively, multi-hop reasoning may just not be a crucial component of many NLP tasks as currently formulated, or LEGO might not be capturing the actual character of chain-based reasoning as required for real-world task.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
See above

Minor:
- Regarding Transformers that repeat layers: in addition to ALBERT, it would be helpful to cite other instances such as Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al)

Introducing mechanisms that emulate these specific attention patterns helps close this gap in performance without the need for expensive pre-training. Models struggle with extrapolating to longer chains of reasoning than required during training. - The proposed operationalization of requiring length extrapolation is effective at disentangling issues with positional encoding from the question of whether the model can extrapolate to longer chains of reasoning 
- Experiments help provide an understanding for how pre-training, as well as stochastic depth, help improve certain aspects of the models' capabilities

It would not be surprising for language-specific reasoning to not undergo transfer in this case, potentially leaving only the most general of structural patterns, like the attention patterns revealed in this work.

It could be that understanding multi-hop computation in the style of the LEGO task is the key for explaining current behaviors and proposing improvements. Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
See above

Minor:
- Regarding Transformers that repeat layers: in addition to ALBERT, it would be helpful to cite other instances such as Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al)

Summary Of The Review:
My overall recommendation is based on weighing the thorough and well-written exposition/investigation of the LEGO task with the potential downsides of any synthetic benchmark, namely that it may or may not capture the key elements of actual real-world tasks, and can therefore be limited in its impact.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This work introduces a synthetic benchmark task designed to probe multi-hop reasoning capabilities in Transformer models. The authors investigate how pre-trained transformers acquire this task more efficiently than untrained models, tracing this advantage to particular attention mechanisms. They demonstrate that explicitly encoding these attention patterns can substantially narrow the performance gap without requiring pre-training. Additionally, the paper identifies limitations in length generalization beyond training sequences and shows that weight-tied layer architectures combined with stochastic depth training can mitigate this problem.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**
- The LEGO task provides a valuable diagnostic tool for investigating specific aspects of how Transformers execute multi-hop reasoning operations
- The experimental design effectively isolates length generalization challenges from positional encoding confounds, allowing for clearer mechanistic insights
- The empirical analysis illuminates the respective roles of pre-training and stochastic depth in enhancing model performance on this capability
- The finding that hand-designed attention patterns achieve comparable performance to learned patterns on this task is noteworthy
- The manuscript is well-organized and clearly presented throughout

**Weaknesses:**
- A fundamental property of attention mechanisms is their flexibility to acquire whatever attention patterns a given task demands, with multi-head architectures providing the additional capacity to determine which patterns to apply at which layers. The fact that fixed attention patterns suffice for the LEGO task appears somewhat expected given the task's design. While preliminary results suggest these patterns may transfer to masked language modeling and SQuAD, this generalization question remains insufficiently investigated. It remains unclear whether all necessary components for genuine NLP tasks are adequately represented.
- The experimental setup for evaluating pre-training may not optimally assess pre-training itself, since the protocol involves pre-training on natural language followed by evaluation on synthetic symbolic expressions. Given this setup, it is plausible that language-specific inductive biases fail to transfer, potentially leaving only the most fundamental architectural patterns—such as the attention mechanisms documented here—as transferable components.
- A critical open question concerns the extent to which the LEGO task actually stresses the important capabilities of Transformers. The significance of multi-hop reasoning in the LEGO formulation for explaining and improving real Transformer behavior remains uncertain. It is possible that multi-hop reasoning of this form does not constitute a central bottleneck in most contemporary NLP applications, or alternatively, that LEGO does not accurately reflect how chain-based reasoning manifests in authentic tasks.

## Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

See discussion above.

## Minor Issues

- The discussion of weight-tied layer architectures should acknowledge additional relevant work beyond ALBERT, particularly Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al.), which represents another important instance of this design pattern.

## Overall Assessment

The recommendation balances the manuscript's strengths—specifically, its rigorous and accessible investigation of the LEGO task—against the inherent limitations of synthetic benchmarks. The central concern is whether this task genuinely captures the phenomena most relevant to understanding and improving Transformers on realistic problems. Such synthetic tasks risk being either overly narrow in their implications or misaligned with the actual computational challenges posed by real-world applications.

## Rating

**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper provides empirical investigation of transformer by constructing a synthetic reasoning task -- LEGO (Learning Equality and Group Operations). The LEGO tasks consist of group operations and a chain of reasoning. The major purpose of the synthetic experiment is to provide a controlled setting and unveil what transformer learns. To do so, the paper conduct a series of experiments on the both classic setting (training and testing distribution are the same) and the OOD setting (out of distribution) and they found

(1) Classical generalization happens all the architecture.

(2) OOD generalization depends on the architecture and data preparation.

(3) The non-extrapolating models (architecture fails for OOD) learns short-cut solution.

The author also propose hardcode attention layer that incorporates knowledge over the dataset that seems to work well on LEGO.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength. The paper provides solid contribution towards understanding the Transformer architecture. By constructing a synthetic benchmark, it provides a series experiment that provide new insight and confirm existing observations. 

Weakness. There is no major weakness.

Minor comment. The related work section can be improved, in particular, there is a previous line of work "unveils" transformer over regular language ( in particular dyck language) that should be cited and discussed. Examples include [1] (and see the reference therein), which studies the dyck language and provide some theory and empirical investigation on what transformer learns.

[1] Self-Attention Networks Can Process Bounded Hierarchical Languages. Shunyu Yao, Binghui Peng, Christos Papadimitriou, Karthik Narasimhan. ACL'2021
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Yes
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper provides solid contribution to the literature and I vote for acceptance.


Some minor questions over "shortcut":
The paper finds transformer sometime learns short-cut solution to the reasoning tasks. However, it is unclear to me why this is a bad thing. I feel it is not equivalent to spurious feature because some reasoning tasks involves short-cut solution or parallelizable solution, e.g., it is possible that a linear size circuit only has depth $O(\log n)$ (i.e., do not follow the chain of reasoning). Some further discussions should be a good addition on the finding!



------------

Post rebuttal

I have read the rebuttal and my evaluation remains.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This work presents an empirical analysis of transformer architectures through the design of a controlled synthetic task called LEGO (Learning Equality and Group Operations), which combines group-theoretic operations with sequential reasoning chains. The authors conduct comprehensive experiments across two distinct settings—standard in-distribution evaluation and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization—to characterize transformer behavior. Their key findings include: (1) all tested architectures achieve satisfactory generalization under standard conditions, (2) OOD generalization performance varies significantly across architectural choices and data construction strategies, and (3) architectures that fail to generalize out-of-distribution tend to exploit shortcut solutions. Additionally, the authors introduce a hardcoded attention mechanism that incorporates dataset-specific inductive biases, demonstrating improved performance on the LEGO benchmark.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths.** The paper makes a meaningful contribution to our understanding of transformer behavior. Through the design of a well-controlled synthetic benchmark, the authors systematically investigate transformer learning dynamics and provide both novel insights and empirical validation of existing theoretical predictions.

**Weaknesses.** No significant weaknesses are identified.

**Minor Issues.** The related work section would benefit from more comprehensive coverage. Specifically, prior research investigating transformer capabilities on formal languages—particularly work examining Dyck language recognition—deserves attention and discussion. The authors should cite and engage with relevant prior work in this area, such as [1], which provides both theoretical analysis and empirical investigation of transformer learning on hierarchical language tasks.

[1] Yao, S., Peng, B., Papadimitriou, C., & Narasimhan, K. (2021). Self-Attention Networks Can Process Bounded Hierarchical Languages. In *Proceedings of ACL 2021*.

## Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

All aspects are satisfactory.

## Overall Assessment

The paper represents a solid contribution to the field and merits publication.

### Minor Clarification Requested

Regarding the observation that non-generalizing models learn shortcut solutions: The authors identify this phenomenon but the review raises a conceptual question about its interpretation. Specifically, it remains somewhat unclear whether shortcut solutions should be viewed negatively in all contexts. The distinction between problematic spurious features and legitimate efficient solutions deserves clarification. For instance, many reasoning tasks admit efficient solutions with logarithmic depth despite linear task length, suggesting that bypassing sequential reasoning chains may sometimes be computationally justified rather than indicative of failure. The authors would strengthen their analysis by elaborating on when shortcut solutions represent genuine learning failures versus when they constitute valid alternative solution strategies.

---

## Post-Rebuttal

The author's response has been reviewed, and the original evaluation stands unchanged.

## Rating

**8: Accept, good paper**

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This work introduces LEGO, a synthetic benchmark designed to assess chain-of-thought reasoning capabilities in neural networks, drawing inspiration from Markov chain principles where each state depends solely on its predecessor. The authors conduct a comparative analysis of BERT and ALBERT, with particular attention to ALBERT's weight-sharing mechanism across layers, which they hypothesize may provide advantages for this type of sequential reasoning task. The empirical findings support this hypothesis, with ALBERT demonstrating superior performance. The paper includes several ablation studies aimed at elucidating the underlying architectural factors driving these performance differences.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

The paper presents its core task and experimental framework in a well-structured manner. The ablation studies—including investigations of stochastic depth, length extrapolation, pretraining effects, and probing classifier analysis—represent thoughtful methodological choices for dissecting the inductive biases embedded in these architectures.

However, the reasoning task itself appears to lack specificity to transformer-based models. A notable limitation is the absence of comparative evaluation against recurrent architectures; it remains unclear whether RNNs or similar sequential models might exhibit comparable or even superior performance on this benchmark.

## Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

The paper is generally well-written and accessible. While the incorporation of abstract algebra terminology strengthens the mathematical framing, this may limit comprehension among some readers. The visualizations are effective and informative. The experimental methodology is sound and appears sufficiently detailed to enable reproduction by other researchers. The modeling approaches themselves are not particularly novel, though the task design and accompanying analytical framework offer meaningful contributions to the literature.

## Overall Assessment

This work makes a valuable contribution to our understanding of the distinct inductive biases present across different transformer variants, particularly in the context of mathematical reasoning tasks—a topic of growing research interest. However, the evaluation could be substantially strengthened by including additional model architectures in the benchmark suite.

## Rating

**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

ALBERT is particularly interesting since weights are shared across all layers in ALBERT and is hypothesized that it may be particularly suited for this type of reasoning. Indeed, it outperforms BERT. Various ablations are carried out to understand why.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The task and setup are framed well. Ablations such as stochastic depth length extrapolation, pretraining, and probing classifier are creative ways at understanding the inductive biases of the networks.

The task seems not that specific to transformers. I do wonder how other RNNs would fare.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
I found the paper clear. I appreciated the language of abstract algebra, but I am not sure if all readers will. I thought the figures were good. The experiments are well done and should be simple to reproduce. There is not much originality in the modeling, but the task and analysis are novel

Summary Of The Review:
I think the paper adds to the understanding of the various inductive biases that different transformer architectures have. There is much interest int the mathematical ability of transformers. I would like to see more models benchmarked on this task.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a synthetic task, LEGO, and uses it to understand how Transformers operate. The task is in following a chain of simple reasoning, in particular for simple mathematical operations with variables valued either +1 or -1 and expressions of the kind a=+b or a=-b. With this task, the authors consider BERT’s and ALBERT’s abilities to generalize to longer inputs. They find specific attention heads (e.g., local attention patterns) and propose modification with some of the heads fixed to these patterns.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
While the paper introduces the new task to help understanding Transformers, the task does not lead to new observations not widely known before. Specifically, it’s well-known that the most important Transformer attention heads play interpretable roles, including positional ([1], [2], [3], etc). This was used extensively to improve/simplify models: [4] fixes attention window size for some of the heads and gets improvement for low-resource settings (similar to this work), [5] develops hard-coded attention without learned parameters, to name a few. From this perspective, (1) neither finding these attention patterns nor proposing fixed attention is novel; (2) doing it on a synthetic task when this has already been done before for different real-world tasks does not make much sense.


[1] ACL 2019: Analyzing Multi-Head Self-Attention: Specialized Heads Do the Heavy Lifting, the Rest Can Be Pruned.

[2] EMNLP 2019: ADaptively Sparse Transformers.

[3] BlackBoxNLP 2019: What does BERT Look at? An Analysis of BERT’s Attention

[4] EMNLP findings 2020: Fixed Encoder Self-Attention Patterns in Transformer-Based Machine Translation

[5] ACL 2020: Hard-Coded Gaussian Attention for Neural Machine Translation
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: See comments above.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper introduces a synthetic task to analyse Transformers. Most of the observations done in the paper and model modification have already been done before without resorting to synthetic tasks.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a synthetic task, LEGO, and uses it to understand how Transformers operate. The task is in following a chain of simple reasoning, in particular for simple mathematical operations with variables valued either +1 or -1 and expressions of the kind a=+b or a=-b. With this task, the authors consider BERT’s and ALBERT’s abilities to generalize to longer inputs. They find specific attention heads (e.g., local attention patterns) and propose modification with some of the heads fixed to these patterns.

Strength And Weaknesses:
While the paper introduces the new task to help understanding Transformers, the task does not lead to new observations not widely known before. Specifically, it’s well-known that the most important Transformer attention heads play interpretable roles, including positional ([1], [2], [3], etc). This was used extensively to improve/simplify models: [4] fixes attention window size for some of the heads and gets improvement for low-resource settings (similar to this work), [5] develops hard-coded attention without learned parameters, to name a few. From this perspective, (1) neither finding these attention patterns nor proposing fixed attention is novel; (2) doing it on a synthetic task when this has already been done before for different real-world tasks does not make much sense.


[1] ACL 2019: Analyzing Multi-Head Self-Attention: Specialized Heads Do the Heavy Lifting, the Rest Can Be Pruned.

[2] EMNLP 2019: ADaptively Sparse Transformers.

[3] BlackBoxNLP 2019: What does BERT Look at? An Analysis of BERT’s Attention

[4] EMNLP findings 2020: Fixed Encoder Self-Attention Patterns in Transformer-Based Machine Translation

[5] ACL 2020: Hard-Coded Gaussian Attention for Neural Machine Translation

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
See comments above.

The paper introduces a synthetic task to analyse Transformers. In the analysis, finds specific attention patterns and proposes model modification with some of the heads being fixed. Most of the observations done in the paper and model modification have already been done before without resorting to synthetic tasks.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Overview

This work introduces a controlled experimental framework called LEGO to investigate Transformer behavior. The authors focus on a simplified domain involving variable assignments and arithmetic operations (using values of +1 or -1), and examine how BERT and ALBERT generalize to sequences longer than those seen during training. The study identifies particular attention heads exhibiting interpretable patterns and proposes constraining these heads to fixed attention mechanisms as a potential improvement strategy.

## Strengths and Limitations

The paper's central contribution—developing a synthetic benchmark to probe Transformer mechanisms—is conceptually sound. However, the specific findings and proposed modifications lack sufficient novelty given existing literature. The observation that Transformer models contain interpretable, specialized attention heads is well-established in prior work (see references [1], [2], [3]). Moreover, the idea of fixing attention patterns in certain heads has already been explored and validated on realistic tasks in previous studies ([4], [5]). Consequently, the paper faces two significant issues: (1) identifying interpretable attention heads and proposing fixed attention mechanisms are not novel contributions, and (2) conducting these investigations on a synthetic task when analogous work has already been performed on authentic real-world applications diminishes the added value of this approach.

## Assessment of Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

The manuscript is generally well-presented, but the limited novelty of both the findings and the proposed modifications substantially undermines its contribution. The core insights regarding attention head specialization and the benefits of constraining attention patterns have been previously demonstrated without relying on synthetic experimental settings.

## Summary

The paper presents a synthetic task designed to understand Transformer operations and identifies interpretable attention patterns that could be fixed to improve model performance. However, these observations and the associated model modifications represent incremental advances over established prior work. The decision to employ a synthetic task does not sufficiently differentiate or advance the field when similar investigations have already been conducted on more realistic datasets and applications.

## Recommendation

**3: Reject – The contribution is insufficient for publication**

The work would require substantial additional insights or a clearer demonstration of advantages over existing approaches to warrant acceptance.

title: Model Transferability with Responsive Decision Subjects

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper formulates a very interesting and novel problem (IDA, induced domain adapatation) in transfer learning. Consider the supervised classification setting where one usually trains a classifier $h : X \mapsto Y$ from samples $\{(X, Y)\}$ drawn from some distribution $\sim \mathcal D$. Oftentimes, when the data are generated from human input, the human could possibly modify $(X, Y)$ to adapt to $h$, resulting in a distribution shift over $\mathcal D$. Therefore, when training $h$, it is important to take this distribution shift into consideration. However, this can become complicated and interactive if the human further adapt to $h$.

This paper conducts a rather detailed study on this problem. It proves upper and lower bounds for the transfer risks for several important fundamental questions in this setting, as outlined in page 2. Besides, the paper realizes their bounds by both showing how to compute them in practice and computing them on real datasets.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
### Strength

1. The setting studied in this paper is important, interesting, and novel.
2. This paper studies most fundamental questions in this setting and presents satisfactory results that covers several fundamental lower and upper bounds in this setting.
3. As a mainly theoretical paper, this paper is aware of the practical impact of their theoretical results. To this end, this paper shows how to compute their upper bounds in real-world problems and conducts experiments to demonstrate their results. Furthermore, the experimental results corroborates their theoretical results and suggests that the theoretical results could give meaningful upper and lower bounds for the transfer risks.

### Weakness

1. The proof techniques look simple and the techniques themselves might not inspire broader community.
2. The authors could possibly conduct experiments on more datasets to strengthen their experimental results.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Overview

The paper introduces an intriguing and previously unexplored problem within the transfer learning domain: induced domain adaptation (IDA). The core scenario examines supervised classification where a classifier $h : X \mapsto Y$ is trained on data $\{(X, Y)\}$ sampled from distribution $\sim \mathcal D$. A key observation is that when humans generate data, they may strategically modify $(X, Y)$ in response to the classifier $h$, thereby inducing shifts in the underlying distribution $\mathcal D$. The challenge becomes even more complex when this adaptation becomes reciprocal—the classifier adapts to the humans' modifications, which in turn triggers further human adaptation. This paper provides a comprehensive theoretical and empirical investigation of this phenomenon.

The work contributes through establishing both upper and lower bounds on transfer risk for several core theoretical questions in this setting (as detailed on page 2). Beyond theoretical analysis, the authors demonstrate practical applicability by deriving computational procedures for their upper bounds and validating their findings on real-world datasets.

## Strengths

1. The problem formulation is timely, well-motivated, and represents a novel contribution to the transfer learning literature.
2. The paper addresses fundamental questions within this framework and delivers solid theoretical contributions, including comprehensive coverage of upper and lower bounds for transfer risk.
3. Despite being primarily theoretical in nature, the paper demonstrates commendable awareness of practical relevance. The authors provide concrete algorithms for computing their upper bounds in real applications and validate their theoretical predictions through experiments. The empirical findings align well with the theoretical results, suggesting that the derived bounds offer meaningful and actionable estimates of transfer risk.

## Weaknesses

1. The mathematical techniques employed are relatively straightforward, and the proof methodology is unlikely to generate significant methodological insights for the broader research community.
2. The experimental validation would benefit from evaluation across a more extensive collection of datasets, which would strengthen the empirical support for the theoretical claims.

## Assessment of Presentation, Novelty, and Reproducibility

The manuscript is clearly written with no notable technical errors. The authors have thoughtfully provided code and supplementary materials to facilitate reproduction of their experimental findings.

## Concluding Remarks

The paper presents solid theoretical contributions paired with meaningful empirical validation. However, the relative simplicity of the underlying mathematical techniques and the limited scope of the experimental evaluation prevent it from being a strong accept. The work makes a valuable contribution to an important problem area, though with some limitations in technical innovation and experimental comprehensiveness.

## Decision

**8: accept, good paper**

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

### Paper Summary

This work investigates the question of model transferability in settings where human decision-makers respond adaptively to deployed predictive models. The authors establish a collection of lower and upper bounds characterizing the gap between the empirical risk minimizer and the theoretically optimal classifier, with particular attention to two prevalent distribution shift scenarios: covariate shift and target shift.

### Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**
The manuscript demonstrates strong presentation quality, with well-articulated problem motivation and logical flow throughout. The theoretical development is supported by clear notation and accessible exposition of the main results, which facilitates reader comprehension.

**Weaknesses:**
A significant limitation is that, as acknowledged by the authors in Section 3.3, the theoretical bounds derived in this work are primarily of academic interest without demonstrated practical relevance. The paper would benefit substantially from a rigorous analysis of bound tightness—even a single concrete example demonstrating when these bounds are achieved would strengthen the contribution considerably. Additionally, the mathematical techniques employed, while correct, do not represent particularly novel or sophisticated methodological advances.

### Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

The paper is generally well-written and accessible. However, my primary concern relates to the practical utility of the derived bounds. Specifically, I would appreciate clarification on the following points:

1. Under what conditions, if any, are the proposed bounds tight for the empirical risk minimizer h_S? Can the authors provide evidence of tightness, even in asymptotic regimes?

2. Given the authors' statement in Section 6 regarding "the suboptimality of training on D_S," do alternative learning algorithms yield improved bounds? One might reasonably expect that incorporating regularization penalties would produce superior theoretical guarantees.

While addressing these questions may introduce additional assumptions, establishing tightness results—whether exact or asymptotic—would substantially enhance the contribution's value.

### Summary

The paper provides a collection of bounds characterizing model transferability, and the proofs I examined (though not exhaustively) appear sound. However, the work would be considerably strengthened by deeper investigation into bound tightness. In the general case presented, even the non-negativity of the lower bound remains ambiguous, which raises questions about the bounds' meaningfulness.

### Rating

**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: In this paper, the authors explore what guarantees we can identify on the performance of classifiers in response to classification subjects which act strategically - that is, attempt to update their inputs so as to achieve a better output. They call this loss the "induced risk": the risk the model incurs on the input distribution which its deployment induces. Their results are mostly of two forms: upper bounds on the induced risk above the optimal induced risk, and lower bounds on the minimum of the source risk and the induced risk. The machinery used for their proofs is mostly domain-adaptation-inspired, where the source distribution is the original data, and the target distribution is the strategically modified data.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- These are useful results and I think more general than what has previously been shown in strategic classification
- paper is mostly pretty clear and exposition is good

Feedback: 
-Sec 4 and 5: it isn't clear to me why Covariate/Target shift would be useful assumptions for the strategic classification setting. One would imagine that the induced distribution will have a much lower P(Y = 1 | X) for X which are right over the decision boundary, for instance; or that P(X | Y = 1) would cluster more closely near the decision boundary in the induced distribution. I'm happy to be convinced otherwise, but it seems to me that by the definition of strategic classification, these results aren't so useful.
-Sec 4.2: it would be good to explain why these might be natural assumptions: I think I understand it has something to do with people choosing to move their inputs towards the desirable outcome Y=1, but not totally sure
Sec 4.3, Setup 3/4: I think there needs to be a little more definition around how the adapted feature is generated. For instance, if \tau = 0.5, x = 0.4, and B = 0.2, then the probability of a successful update is 0.5, but it is drawn from the distribution U(0.5, 0,3), which doesn't obviously make sense to me.
-Prop 4.7: I don't quite understand why as Err_D_S(h*_T) goes up in this bound, the gap increases - it would be nice to get some intuition on this relationship
Replicator Dynamics: It would be good to have a better description of this in the body of the paper, I'm not so clear on exactly how this works
Experiments: I don't totally understand the data generation process, even after looking at the supplement. It would be good to have a clearer explanation of the exact strategic modification model here (e.g. what is \epsilon or \alpha, as defined in the supplement)

Notes:
Eq (3) - would be good to define the optimal model here: it's not clear at this point in the paper if h*_T is the model with the optimal induced risk, or optimal source risk (you explain later, but should explain here)
Sec 4.2 - should define X_+(h) and X_-(h)
Sec 5: a little bit confusing to me to overload w here as the induced positive rate, when it was the weighting coefficient earlier
Thm 5.2: what is p here?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: decent clarity around the exposition and proofs, some confusion around replicator dynamics and experiments
Quality: I think the work is solid, however I'm not sure that the assumptions relied on by the proofs are all appropriate
Novelty: I think this is novel because it uses lighter assumptions (mostly) than previous strategic classification work
Reproducibility: I have a little confusion around the experiments at the moment, with a bit more information this could be reproducible
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, this paper provides some useful results in the strategic classification space. I have some doubts about the applicability of the covariate shift/label shift assumptions to this setting, and therefore not sure how useful those results are. Additionally, I have some confusion around other assumptions made around the data generative process with replicator dynamics and the experiments. Due to some good results and exposition alongside these concerns, I'm recommending a Weak Reject for now.

In this paper, the authors explore what guarantees we can identify on the performance of classifiers in response to classification subjects which act strategically - that is, attempt to update their inputs so as to achieve a better output. They call this loss the "induced risk": the risk the model incurs on the input distribution which its deployment induces. Their results are mostly of two forms: upper bounds on the induced risk above the optimal induced risk, and lower bounds on the minimum of the source risk and the induced risk. The machinery used for their proofs is mostly domain-adaptation-inspired, where the source distribution is the original data, and the target distribution is the strategically modified data.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- These are useful results and I think more general than what has previously been shown in strategic classification
- paper is mostly pretty clear and exposition is good

Feedback: 
-Sec 4 and 5: it isn't clear to me why Covariate/Target shift would be useful assumptions for the strategic classification setting. One would imagine that the induced distribution will have a much lower P(Y = 1 | X) for X which are right over the decision boundary, for instance; or that P(X | Y = 1) would cluster more closely near the decision boundary in the induced distribution. I'm happy to be convinced otherwise, but it seems to me that by the definition of strategic classification, these results aren't so useful.
-Sec 4.2: it would be good to explain why these might be natural assumptions: I think I understand it has something to do with people choosing to move their inputs towards the desirable outcome Y=1, but not totally sure
Sec 4.3, Setup 3/4: I think there needs to be a little more definition around how the adapted feature is generated. For instance, if \tau = 0.5, x = 0.4, and B = 0.2, then the probability of a successful update is 0.5, but it is drawn from the distribution U(0.5, 0,3), which doesn't obviously make sense to me.
-Prop 4.7: I don't quite understand why as Err_D_S(h*_T) goes up in this bound, the gap increases - it would be nice to get some intuition on this relationship
Replicator Dynamics: It would be good to have a better description of this in the body of the paper, I'm not so clear on exactly how this works
Experiments: I don't totally understand the data generation process, even after looking at the supplement. It would be good to have a clearer explanation of the exact strategic modification model here (e.g. what is \epsilon or \alpha, as defined in the supplement)

Notes:
Eq (3) - would be good to define the optimal model here: it's not clear at this point in the paper if h*_T is the model with the optimal induced risk, or optimal source risk (you explain later, but should explain here)
Sec 4.2 - should define X_+(h) and X_-(h)
Sec 5: a little bit confusing to me to overload w here as the induced positive rate, when it was the weighting coefficient earlier
Thm 5.2: what is p here?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: decent clarity around the exposition and proofs, some confusion around replicator dynamics and experiments
Quality: I think the work is solid, however I'm not sure that the assumptions relied on by the proofs are all appropriate
Novelty: I think this is novel because it uses lighter assumptions (mostly) than previous strategic classification work
Reproducibility: I have a little confusion around the experiments at the moment, with a bit more information this could be reproducible

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, this paper provides some useful results in the strategic classification space. I have some doubts about the applicability of the covariate shift/label shift assumptions to this setting, and therefore not sure how useful those results are. Additionally, I have some confusion around other assumptions made around the data generative process with replicator dynamics and the experiments. Due to some good results and exposition alongside these concerns, I'm recommending a Weak Reject for now.
